data.title: Grijalva says ‘very aggressive’ ICE agent pepper-sprayed her during Tuscon raid
data.description:
Rep. Adelita Grijalva (D-Az.) on Friday said a “very aggressive” Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent pepper-sprayed her during a raid in Tuscon, Arizona. 

According to Grijalva, community members in Tuscon had stopped approximately 40 ICE agents, most of whom were masked, in the middle of a street near Taco Giro, which Grijalva described as a small mom-and-pop restaurant 

She said the community members stopped the officers “because they were afraid they [the agents] were taking people without due process.”

“I was here — this is like the restaurant I come to literally once a week — and was sprayed in the face by a very aggressive agent, pushed around by others, when I literally was not being aggressive,” Grijalva said in a video posted to social platform X. “I was asking for clarification, which is my right as a member of Congress.”

ICE conducted raids at multiple restaurants across Tucson on early Friday morning. The raids ended with multiple individuals in custody, according to a public statement made by an ICE spokesperson.

In a press release, Grijalva noted her staff had also been pushed and pepper-sprayed.

“While I am fine, if that is the way they treat me, how are they treating other community members who do not have the same privileges and protections that I do?” Grijalva said in the release. 

She went on to call ICE a “lawless agency,” echoing widespread criticism from Democrats over the Trump administration’s increasingly aggressive immigration enforcement operations. Raids across the country — including in Chicago and California — have led to clashes between agents and people protesting their operations or detainment of community members. 

“ICe has become a lawless agency under this Administration — operating with no transparency, no accountability, and open disregard for basic due process,” she said. “No family in our community should live in fear, and I will not rest until we get clear answers and accountability.”

data.title: Gun laws may end up in the courts, but they start with Congress
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court didn’t just reject Mexico’s $10 billion lawsuit against American gun makers — it obliterated it.  

A unanimous 9–0 decision in Smith & Wesson v. Mexico confirmed what Congress made clear two decades ago: The Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act of 2005 bars attempts to blame gun manufacturers for gun crimes committed by third parties.  

Yet judging by his recent op-ed, Jonathan Lowy, the lawyer behind that spectacular failure, is back for an encore — this time targeting Arizona gun retailers. His Nov. 30 op-ed in The Hill, with co-author Pablo Arrocha Olabuenaga, also pitches Mexico as a global champion against gun violence. He seems to be hoping it will distract media and especially donor attention away from the legal brick wall into which he just sprinted headlong. 

Congress passed the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act to stop activist-driven lawsuits intended to bankrupt gun-makers for crimes they did not commit and for which they were not responsible. The statute codifies basic tort principles — for example, that liability requires a direct link between a defendant’s unlawful act and the harm alleged.  

The lawful manufacture and sale of firearms that are later trafficked by criminals across borders do not meet that standard. The Supreme Court spelled this out quite clearly. Mexico’s claim, Justice Elena Kagan wrote, “closely resembles the ones Congress had in mind” when it enacted the 2005 law. 

Lowy knows this, because he helped engineer the original wave of such lawsuits in the late 1990s and early 2000s. Back then, big-city mayors and trial lawyers were conspiring to sue gun makers into submission. Congress shut that down with bipartisan fervor. The 2005 federal law did not create “sweeping immunity,” as Lowy now claims, but it did reaffirm common sense. 

Legal scholars broadly agree with this perspective. Victor Schwartz, author of the leading tort law textbook, calls the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act “a common-sense law that protects against unsound attempts to change liability principles.” Jonathan Turley of The George Washington University notes that even without this statute, courts have “uniformly and correctly” rejected these types of claims. 

Lowy’s assertion that lawsuits could curb illegal trafficking “where other efforts have failed” collapses under scrutiny. Every retail gun sale already requires an FBI background check and completion of ATF Form 4473, wherein buyers attest that they are not straw-purchasers. To lie on that form is a federal felony punishable by 15 years in prison. For 25 years, the firearms industry has partnered with ATF on the “Don’t Lie for the Other Guy” campaign to warn the public about these penalties. 

The more risible claim is that Mexico’s government is some kind of crusader against gun violence. In fact, former President Andres Manuel López Obrador’s “hugs, not bullets” approach was a boon for the cartels, which have funneled millions into political campaigns, according to evidence uncovered by the DEA. His successor, Claudia Sheinbaum, hasn’t broken that pattern. Earlier this year, she shared a stage with Salvador Cienfuegos, a former defense minister arrested by U.S. authorities in 2020 for cartel ties and then controversially handed over to Mexico.  

The lawsuit racket against lawful gun vendors and manufacturers is really just an end-around to our democratic process — a way of imposing gun control through courts after such measures failed in legislatures. It won’t work because we have laws against such sneak-attacks on gun rights.  

If you want new gun laws, you need to take your case to Congress, not the courthouse. 

Lawrence Keane is senior vice president of the National Shooting Sports Foundation.

data.title: GOP North Carolina senator lifts holds on DHS nominees after FEMA funds approved for his state
data.description:
Sen. Ted Budd (R-N.C.) said Friday he will lift his remaining holds on President Trump’s nominees to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) after the department approved disaster recovery funds for his state.

Budd had holds on Sean Plankey, who was nominated to be director of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, and Pedro Allende, who was nominated to be under secretary for Science and Technology.

His announcement comes after the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) approved $29 million in reimbursements for Hurricane Helene recovery projects in North Carolina.

He previously lifted his hold on James Percival, who was nominated to be the agency’s general counsel after FEMA OK’ed another $155 million.

“I am grateful for Secretary [Kristi] Noem working with me to more regularly approve reimbursements for Helene and past disaster recovery projects,” Budd said in a statement Friday. 

However, he also noted that there’s still more funding that has yet to be approved.

“While I have released my holds on DHS nominees, I will continue to engage all relevant federal agencies to make sure Western North Carolina receives the focus and attention it deserves, particularly the final distribution of funds to the municipalities and state agencies in desperate need of financial relief,” he said. 

Budd announced the holds earlier this year, at the time, he criticized a policy requiring Noem to personally approve all expenditures over $100,000. It’s a move that the Trump administration argues will save taxpayers money, but critics say will cause delays for disaster response and recovery.

“I know that in each county, every item almost is over $100,000. That’s every single thing that runs through a rather significant agency. I just don’t think that’s the way you navigate this. I think there are very competent, even Senate-approved people that can share the load and approve things,” Budd told CNN in September. “Choke holding this thing or stonewalling states that are hurt by hurricanes is not the way to get rid of waste fraud and abuse.”

Under its Public Assistance Program, FEMA provides grants to help states, tribes, local governments and some nonprofits to respond to major disasters. These grants fund projects like debris removal and restoring infrastructure. 

The Trump administration has taken a critical eye to FEMA. Noem and president Trump have floated axing the agency entirely, though they are expected to ultimately shrink its role rather than eliminate it entirely.

The review council set up by administration is expected to soon publish a list of policy reform recommendations.

data.title: Erika Kirk co-hosting ‘The Five’ as book tour launches
data.description:
Erika Kirk, the widow of Turning Point USA founder Charlie Kirk, is slated to co-host Fox News’s “The Five” next week in an effort to promote the release of her late husband’s book “STOP, in the Name of God.”

The book was finished a month prior to his death on the campus of Utah Valley University. After his assassination, Erika Kirk took over as CEO of Turning Point USA and vowed to expand the organization at college campuses across the country. 

Kirk’s death catapulted his wife into the spotlight as the public sought to learn more about her late husband’s views on politics and policy.

Erika will co-host Fox News’s “The Five” on Dec. 9 after an appearance on “Fox & Friends” earlier in the day. 

The media blitz will continue with a sit-down discussion on Will Cain’s podcast, “Will Cain Country,” in addition to a CBS News primetime town hall on Dec. 13 hosted by editor-in-chief Bari Weiss.

“Like so many people around the world, I will never forget the moment that Erika Kirk forgave her husband’s killer,” Weiss said in a statement shared by The Guardian. 

“I am eager to speak to her — and thrilled to be doing so in front of a group of Americans who I know will elevate the conversation,” she added.

In addition to speaking about her late husband’s new book, Kirk is expected to address the topic of gun violence and political partisanship.

“What I’ve realized through all of this is that you can have individuals that will always resort to violence,” she previously told CNBC host Andrew Ross Sorkin. “And what I’m afraid of is that we are living in a day and age where they think violence is the solution to them not wanting to hear a different point of view.”

Kirk continued, “That’s not a gun problem, that’s a human — deeply human — problem. That is a soul problem, that is a mental [problem], that is a very deeper issue.”

data.title: DeSantis: ‘Big pool’ of Trump voters an issue for GOP in midterms
data.description:
Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) said President Trump’s voters may be an issue for the Republican Party during next year’s midterms because this “big pool” of people may not show up to vote without him on the ballot. 

“I think Republicans have an issue that Donald Trump has created a big pool of voters, but some of them are unique to him, right?” DeSantis said Friday on “Fox & Friends.” “So they’ll go vote for Trump, and they’ll vote for all Republicans when Trump is on the ballot. But if he’s not on the ballot, some of them don’t vote.”

He said that this dynamic played out during every midterm election.

“In an off-year midterm, the party in power’s voters tend to be more complacent,” he said. “The party out of power, they get upset, right? That’s just the nature of midterms.”

He cautioned Republicans against thinking that they would win districts that voted for Trump last year, saying these voters had come out for the president and might not for other GOP members running for office.

“You can overcome that,” DeSantis said, pointing to Republican wins in Florida during the 2022 elections. 

DeSantis won his reelection as governor in a landslide victory in 2022, while Republicans statewide flipped four Democratic-held House districts, bringing the number of Florida House Republicans up to 20. The state has 28 House seats. 

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who then served as a senator, also won reelection against Democratic opponent Val Demmings. 

“We won by a million and a half votes, 20 points, the biggest Republican win ever,” DeSantis said. “But we did that based on results and substance. We did it by flying under a banner of bold colors, not pale pastels.

“We were sticking it to the left every single day and delivering big victories,” he continued. “So, it can be done.”

data.title: Florida’s ‘Alligator Alcatraz’ ‘box’ may amount to torture, says Amnesty International
data.description:
Amnesty International on Thursday said the use of a confined space called the “box” at the migrant detention facility in Florida known as Alligator Alcatraz could amount to torture.

The human rights organization said in a 61-page digital report that the box, a 2-by-2-foot cage located in Alligator Alcatraz’s yard, keeps people restrained to the ground and holds them still for hours. Exposure to the heat, insects and the elements puts these prisoners at risk, Amnesty stated.

“The conditions of the ‘box’ … would likely cause severe physical and psychological pain and suffering,” the report read. “Amnesty International considers that the use of the ‘box’ as a form of punishment at ‘Alligator Alcatraz’ constitutes torture.”

Four men who said they were subject to punishment in the cage told Amnesty about its conditions. Amnesty made these and other findings during a trip to the detention facility in September.

The Hill reached out to the White House and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for comment.

Amnesty’s report also concluded that people there were held in “inhuman and unsanitary conditions, including overflowing toilets with fecal matter seeping into where people are sleeping, limited access to showers, exposure to insects without protective measures, lights on 24 hours a day, poor quality food and water, and lack of privacy.”

“Amnesty International also documented the routine and prolonged use of shackles on individuals detained for immigration purposes, both at detention facilities and during transfer between facilities, which constitutes cruel, inhuman and degrading treatment, and may amount to torture or other ill-treatment,” the report stated.

Along with poor conditions, Amnesty noted overcrowding, delays in intake procedures, inadequate and inaccessible medical care and the practice of prolonged solitary confinement. The use of prolonged solitary confinement at “the facility “constitute cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment, and may amount to torture or other ill-treatment,” the report read.

Based on the report’s findings, Amnesty called on the federal government to stop criminalizing immigrants, ban the use of state-owned facilities for immigration custody detention; follow thorough investigations into all deaths, abuses and allegations of torture in custody; and follow international human rights laws and standards.

An appeals court previously paused the dismantling of Alligator Alcatraz after a lower court ordered it be taken down in September. The lower court deemed the facility to be a threat to the environment.

data.title: Survivors of boat strike did not radio for backup, Bradley tells lawmakers: Report
data.description:
The two survivors of the early September boat attack by the U.S. military did not seem to radio for backup or appear to have other communication devices available after their vessel was struck, the top official overseeing the Caribbean operation told lawmakers during Thursday’s classified, closed-door briefings, CNN reported, citing three sources familiar with the matter. 

Adm. Frank Bradley, who is the commander of U.S. Special Operations Command (SoCom), told lawmakers on Capitol Hill that the two survivors of the Sept. 2 attack were not positioned to execute a distress call, the outlet reported. 

The Hill has reached out to SoCom for comment. 

Bradley, a decorated Navy admiral, was at the time of the attack against the alleged drug-trafficking boat — the first lethal U.S. military operation since the counternarcotics campaign kicked off in the region — heading the Joint Special Operations Command and ordered the second strike after the first one killed nine “narco-terrorists.” 

Bradley’s reported acknowledgment to lawmakers during the briefings comes as military officials have argued that the killing of the remaining survivors did not constitute a war crime because, in part, the alleged drug-smugglers seem to have tried to use the radio to call for backup or assistance. If secured, this would, in theory, permit them to resume their drug-trafficking on the capsized vessel.

After the initial strike, which killed most of those aboard and split the vessel in half, U.S. forces, at the direction of Bradley, conducted three more hits, the second one killing the last two survivors. The third and fourth strikes sank the boat, Sen. Chris Coons (D-Del.), who is on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, told reporters Thursday after the briefing. 

Following the first strike, the two survivors attempted to flip the boat and continue on with the mission, Sen. Tom Cotton (R-Ark.), the chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, told reporters. 

Rep. Adam Smith (D-Wash.), the ranking member on the House Armed Services Committee, while appearing Thursday on NewsNation’s “The Hill,” disagreed with Cotton’s characterization of events and said the two survivors were leaning on the bow of the boat, the only part of the vessel that was still sticking out of the water after the first strike. 

“There was no way they were flipping that back over and going anywhere. The boat was totally disabled and floating in the current,” Smith said Thursday evening. 

Smith said that part of the reason Bradley authorized the second strike was that U.S. military officials could not tell if all of the drugs on board, likely cocaine, were destroyed. 

“But that was the basis for the strike, not that they were trying to right-size the boat and go on their merry way. That wasn’t happening. It was, I suppose, arguably possible that someone could come to assist them, but we had eyes on the scene, and nobody was,” the Washington Democrat told host Blake Burman. 

When asked Friday morning on CNN if he saw evidence during the briefing that the two survivors tried to use a radio, Cotton said “no,” but emphasized that the alleged drug-smugglers were “clearly not incapacitated.” 

The Arkansas senator said the survivors were not “distressed” and that one of them took his t-shirt off after the strike as he was “sunbathing.” 

“They were trying to get the boat back up and to continue their mission of spreading these drugs all across America,” Cotton said. 

Following the briefings on Thursday, Democrats said they were disturbed after watching the video of the Sept. 2 operation, with Rep. Jim Himes (Conn.), the top Democrat on the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence, saying the evidence indicated that the U.S. troops targeted the two survivors who did not pose a threat to U.S. security. 

“I reviewed the video, and it’s deeply, deeply troubling,” Himes said on Thursday. “The fact is that we killed two people who were in deep distress and had neither the means nor obviously the intent to continue their mission.”

House Intelligence Chair Rick Crawford (R-Ark.) agreed with Cotton’s characterization of events, saying the survivors tried to flip back the vessel that still contained illicit narcotics since the drugs were “strapped into” it. 

“They were potentially signaling other vessels in the region,” Crawford said during his appearance on Fox News, adding that “we don’t know if they had access to SATCOMs or not, but as he characterized, still in the fight.”
